design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore top-down overworld settlements to gather information and items, then enter dungeons with closer camera view to solve puzzles and defeat enemies to progress through chapters
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Navigation between peaceful exploration and dangerous dungeon combat; discovery and puzzle-solving as gating mechanism
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Teenage hero on tropical island adventure rescuing uncle while uncovering alien mystery; American baseball-themed combat aesthetic
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Zelda-inspired but distinctly American in flavor (baseball elements, teenage protagonist from Seattle); controversial design choice to include physical letter requiring water-dipping to reveal mandatory progression code (747); refuses convention by never releasing in Japan despite Nintendo pedigree

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Progression gated by external puzzle (dipped letter code) rather than in-game difficulty tuning; combat and navigation present escalating challenge through chapters

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon unlocks including yo-yo (star), baseball-themed tools and weapons acquired as player progresses through chapters
progression.unlock_mechanism: Chapter-based progression; mandatory story code (747 from dipped letter) blocks late-game access to alien spaceship and final confrontation
replayability_hooks: Multiple weapon types to experiment with
Hidden level select accessible via Game Genie debug code

world_building_techniques: NPC dialogue in settlements to gather story information and local mission context
Physical letter packaging with game (original NES) providing plot setup and atmosphere
Environmental discovery (message bottle from uncle on island)
Character encounters (parrot, dolphin, whale sequences) establishing world variety
Cutscene delivery of major plot revelations (alien origin, Argonian refugees)

notable_bosses: Zoda - alien leader, final spaceship confrontation; serves as climactic antagonist requiring recovered magic cubes to defeat

faction_indicators: Argonian refugees seeking Earth refuge (post-game integration with village)
Alien invaders as unified threat driving plot
danger_signals: Missing uncle Dr. Steven Jones (abduction setup)
Monsters and obstacles in dungeons escalate threat perception
Spaceship infiltration as high-stakes endgame confrontation
environmental_storytelling: Tropical island settlements suggesting peaceful civilization disrupted
Dungeon architecture reflecting local calamities (whale sequences, ruins)
Submarine as mobility tool anchoring player to island-hopping exploration
Alien escape pod wreckage revealing catastrophic history
